planting.note: Plant the pseudobulbs in spring once hard frost has passed.

directions: Set the corm-like pseudobulbs about two inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil with the growing tip pointing up. Keep evenly moist through the growing season and mulch the crown in colder zones for winter protection. Pleated leaves emerge in spring and arching sprays of magenta-purple flowers open in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant the pseudobulbs two inches deep in rich, moisture-retentive soil that drains freely. Provide dappled shade in hot climates and keep the soil from drying out while the plant is in leaf. Pure white flowers with a lightly frilled lip appear on wiry stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Bury the pseudobulbs a couple inches deep in loose, organic soil in a sheltered, part-shaded bed. Water regularly during active growth and reduce moisture after the foliage yellows in autumn. Soft primrose-yellow flowers, often flushed maroon on the lip, open a little later than the common purple species.

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant the pseudobulbs two inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil enriched with leaf mold. Keep consistently moist while in leaf and shelter from strong afternoon sun. This vigorous hybrid carries larger, bicolored flowers in soft pink and cream from late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in autumn in a limestone-rich bed.

directions: Nestle the rhizome an inch deep in gritty, alkaline soil amended with limestone chippings and leaf mold. Provide cool roots, morning sun, and afternoon shade. Yellow pouches with maroon spiraling petals appear in late spring on this classic European species.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in autumn in rich bottomland soil.

directions: Plant the rhizome an inch deep in deep, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist, mimicking a streamside floodplain. Give filtered light and protect the emerging shoots from late frosts. The largest of the native slippers bears creamy ivory pouches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn in cool woodland soil.

directions: Set the rhizome about one inch deep in loose, organic, free-draining soil with a cool root run. This is one of the easier lady's slippers for the shade garden and clumps up steadily. Pale pink pouches spotted with deeper rose open above pleated, fan-shaped leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn in deep shade.

directions: Lay the running rhizome an inch below rich, leafy soil in a cool, sheltered, shaded spot. Keep the root zone moist but never waterlogged and top-dress annually with leaf mold. Broad, fan-pleated leaves frame a single pink-tinged pouch in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant divisions in autumn in a humus-rich bed.

directions: Plant the rhizome an inch deep in free-draining, humus-rich soil with a cool root run and morning light. This robust garden hybrid multiplies readily and is among the easiest slippers for beginners. Yellow pouches set against wine-red twisted petals open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in autumn in cool, moist soil.

directions: Set the roots just under the surface in cool, evenly moist, neutral soil rich in leaf mold. This vigorous hybrid tolerates damp ground well and forms generous clumps over time. Soft white pouches flushed with pale pink open in early summer.

planting.note: Pot the pseudobulbs in late winter to early spring as growth begins.

directions: Nestle each pseudobulb with its top third above a gritty, bark-based compost in a shallow pan or sheltered raised bed. Keep just moist as roots form, then water freely in leaf and dry off after the foliage dies back. Solitary pink flowers with a fringed, spotted lip open in early spring, often before the leaf expands.

planting.note: Pot pseudobulbs in early spring as new roots appear.

directions: Press the pseudobulbs into an open, free-draining mix so the upper portion sits proud of the surface. Water sparingly until roots establish, then keep moist through the growing season and rest dry over winter. Rose-purple flowers with a paler, blotched lip open in spring alongside the emerging leaf.

planting.note: Pot pseudobulbs in late winter as growth resumes.

directions: Set the pseudobulbs shallowly in a bark and grit compost with the shoulder exposed, in bright but shaded light. Water freely once in leaf and give a dry, cool rest through winter. This popular grex bears large soft yellow to peach flowers with a boldly marked lip in early spring.

planting.note: Pot pseudobulbs in early spring before leaves expand.

directions: Plant each pseudobulb with two-thirds buried in a gritty, humus-rich mix in a shallow container or sheltered pocket. Keep barely moist until active, then water regularly and dry off after leaf fall. Deep rose-pink flowers with a richly patterned lip open in spring.

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs in early spring in a sheltered rock crevice.

directions: This is the hardiest Pleione and can be grown outdoors in a sharply drained, mossy pocket among rocks. Set pseudobulbs with the tips exposed and protect from winter wet with a pane or overhang. Vivid magenta-pink flowers with a crimson-spotted lip appear in spring and it slowly forms a colony.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in a damp, limy bed.

directions: Set the creeping rhizomes just below the surface in moist, alkaline, low-nutrient soil that never dries out. It naturalizes into a slow-spreading patch in a sunny bog or damp meadow. Delicate white-and-pink lipped flowers with maroon markings open in mid summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist woodland soil.

directions: Plant the vigorous rhizomes an inch deep in rich, reliably moist soil in light shade. This robust hybrid clumps and spreads more freely than the species and tolerates ordinary garden beds if kept damp. Tall stems carry many coppery-pink flowers over a long season in summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in a moist, humus-rich bed.

directions: Plant the palmate tubers two to three inches deep in cool, moisture-retentive, moderately acidic soil. Give bright but not scorching light and keep the ground damp through spring growth. Dense conical spikes of pale lilac to pink flowers with spotted leaves appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in rich, moist soil.

directions: Set the finger-like tubers three inches deep in fertile, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist but drains well. One of the showiest and easiest marsh orchids for the border, it bulks up into a fine clump. Bold, densely packed spikes of deep magenta-pink flowers rise above glossy leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in a damp meadow or bed.

directions: Plant the tubers a few inches deep in moist, neutral to alkaline soil rich in organic matter. It naturalizes readily in damp grass and light shade once settled. Pale pink to lilac flowers marked with darker loops form neat spikes above spotted foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in consistently moist ground.

directions: Bury the tubers two to three inches deep in wet to moist, humus-rich soil at a bog or pond margin. Keep the ground reliably damp and avoid strong fertilizers. Stout spikes of rich purple-pink flowers rise above broad, often spotted leaves in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in deep, moist soil.

directions: Plant the tubers three inches deep in deep, fertile, moisture-retentive soil in sun to light shade. The tallest of the commonly grown marsh orchids, it makes an imposing clump in a damp border. Long, dense spikes of vivid purple-magenta flowers open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, sunny soil.

directions: Set the fleshy roots just below the surface in moist to wet, humus-rich soil in full sun to light shade. This selected clone is vigorous, sweetly vanilla-scented, and easy in a damp border or bog edge. Spiraled columns of small white flowers open from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring or early fall with the crown at soil level.

directions: Plant the fan so the white base of the crown sits just at the soil surface and water in well. This compact gold rebloomer flowers from early summer until frost, so shear spent scapes to keep new buds coming. Divide clumps every three or four years to sustain heavy flowering.

planting.note: Set out in spring for bloom the same season in most gardens.

directions: Plant the fan with roots fanned out and the crown just below the surface. This compact rebloomer opens rich ruby-red flowers with a small yellow throat over a long season. Deadhead regularly and shear tired foliage to freshen the clump.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall in full sun to bring out the bicolor contrast.

directions: Set the division at grade and water deeply as roots take hold. Blooms are boldly bicolored, with rust-orange petals striped yellow alternating with clear yellow sepals. Remove spent scapes after the midsummer flush to keep plants neat.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; partial afternoon shade helps hold the pastel tone.

directions: Firm soil around the crown at grade and keep new plantings watered through the first summer. Tall scapes bear fragrant orchid-lavender flowers with a green-yellow throat. Deadhead daily for a cleaner look and cut scapes down once bloom ends.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in full sun for the whitest flowers.

directions: Set the division with the crown just at the surface and water well to settle the roots. Near-white ruffled flowers with a faint green throat open in early to midsummer. Remove spent blooms and divide the clump when flowering thins.

planting.note: A small grower ideal for the front of a border; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the crown at grade and keep it evenly moist as it settles. This dwarf carries rose-red flowers with a green throat over a long midsummer season. Pinch off finished flowers and shear old scapes to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Plant spring to early summer for reblooming through the season.

directions: Fan the roots into the planting hole and keep the crown near the surface. This compact rebloomer opens purple flowers with a gold throat from early summer onward. Deadhead often to prolong the show and divide crowded clumps in fall.

planting.note: A tall, vigorous variety; give it space at the back of a bed.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water thoroughly to establish the strong root system. Tall scapes hold bright orange-tangerine flowers with a red blush and gold throat in midsummer. Cut scapes down after bloom and lift crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: Plant in full sun; some afternoon shade helps the dark petals hold color.

directions: Plant the division with its crown at the soil surface and mulch to keep roots cool. Large deep purple flowers with a green-gold throat rise on strong scapes in midsummer. Remove spent blooms daily to showcase the dark color and cut scapes when done.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for the best repeat bloom.

directions: Settle the crown at grade and water in well as roots knit into the soil. Strawberry-pink flowers with a rose-red eye and gold edge appear in early summer and rebloom. Deadhead and give a summer feeding to push the second flush.

planting.note: A tall, large-flowered variety; place at the back of the border.

directions: Give this vigorous grower room and set the crown at soil level in rich ground. Big spidery tangerine-orange flowers with twisted petals bloom in mid to late summer. Cut scapes back after flowering and divide when the clump crowds itself.

planting.note: Plant near a path or seating area to enjoy the scent.

directions: Plant the fan with the crown just below the surface and water regularly the first year. Fragrant lemon-yellow flowers rebloom from early summer into fall. Remove spent scapes to keep flushes of new buds forming.

planting.note: Plant in spring for reblooming color through summer and fall.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep evenly moist while establishing. Golden-yellow flowers with a red eyezone rebloom over a long summer season. Shear spent scapes and old foliage to keep the compact clump fresh.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for large early flowers.

directions: Position the crown at soil level and firm the soil around the roots. Large ruffled buff-yellow flowers open early in the daylily season. Deadhead daily and cut scapes to the base once blooming finishes.

planting.note: Plant in full sun; the pale flowers glow at dusk in the garden.

directions: Set the division at grade in well-drained soil and water in thoroughly. Near-white flowers with a green throat appear in early to midsummer. Remove finished blooms and cut scapes down when the flush ends.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a late-season bloomer worth waiting for.

directions: Plant the crown at grade and keep watered until the roots establish. Mustard-yellow flowers carry a wine-purple eyezone and matching picotee edge in late summer. Deadhead as flowers fade and cut scapes when bloom is over.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun; a heavy bloomer and good multiplier.

directions: Fan the roots out and set the crown just at the surface. Cream-yellow flowers with a maroon eyezone bloom heavily in early to midsummer. Deadhead regularly and divide every few years to keep bloom counts high.

planting.note: An old, reliable variety; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply to establish. Rich red flowers with a yellow-green throat bloom from mid into late summer. Snap off spent flowers and cut scapes back once the season ends.

planting.note: A vigorous spreader; plant where it can naturalize freely.

directions: Plant divisions at grade in almost any soil; this tough old form spreads by runners. Double tawny-orange flowers open on tall scapes in midsummer. Contain the roots if you want to limit its spread and cut scapes after bloom.

planting.note: The classic roadside daylily; naturalizes readily in rough ground.

directions: Set divisions at grade in sun or light shade; this sterile clone spreads by roots rather than seed. Rusty-orange flowers appear on branched scapes in midsummer. Give it space or edge the planting to keep it in bounds.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for the crisp eye contrast.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep moist until established. Orchid-lavender flowers show a purple eyezone above a green throat in early to midsummer. Remove spent blooms and lift crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: A dependable small variety; plant spring or early fall.

directions: Plant the crown at grade and water in well. Ruffled canary-yellow flowers with a strong green throat bloom in mid to late summer. Deadhead often and cut scapes to the base when finished.

planting.note: Afternoon shade helps preserve the lavender-blue tones.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep evenly moist during establishment. Lavender flowers with a smoky blue-violet eye and green throat bloom in midsummer. Remove finished flowers and cut scapes after the flush.

planting.note: An award-winning pink; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Fan the roots and set the crown just at the surface. Large ruffled soft-pink flowers with a green throat open in midsummer. Deadhead daily for a clean look and divide when clumps grow crowded.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for well-formed, heavily ruffled flowers.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep the soil moist while establishing. Pink flowers with a green throat and heavy ruffled edge bloom in midsummer. Remove spent blooms and cut scapes back when the season ends.

planting.note: A tall, vigorous grower; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply as roots establish. Cream flowers edged and picoteed in rose-pink bloom on tall scapes in midsummer. Deadhead regularly and cut scapes down after bloom.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; reblooms in warmer regions.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep evenly watered while it settles. Mauve-lavender flowers with a deep purple eye and green throat bloom in early to midsummer. Deadhead to encourage repeat bloom where the season is long.

planting.note: A heavy bloomer and good multiplier; plant spring or fall.

directions: Fan the roots and set the crown just at the surface. Pink flowers with a rose band, yellow-green throat, and gold edge bloom over a long season and often rebloom. Deadhead often and divide every few years to sustain vigor.

planting.note: A dainty pastel; plant spring or fall near the front of a bed.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water in well. Ruffled pale pink flowers with a green throat bloom in early to midsummer. Remove spent flowers and cut scapes down when finished.

planting.note: A tall grower; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply to establish. Large rose-cherry flowers with a lighter midrib and yellow-green throat bloom in midsummer. Deadhead daily and cut scapes to the base after bloom.

planting.note: A large, showy red; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Give this vigorous plant room and set the crown at grade in rich soil. Large ruffled scarlet-red flowers with a gold throat bloom on tall scapes in midsummer. Remove spent flowers and divide when clumps become crowded.

planting.note: Reblooms in warmer zones; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep moist while establishing. Coral to tangerine flowers with ruffled edges and a green throat bloom over a long season. Deadhead often to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: A dramatic large-flowered spider; give it room in the border.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply as roots establish. Huge ruby-red spider flowers with a yellow throat open on tall scapes in early to midsummer. Deadhead spent blooms and cut scapes back after flowering.

planting.note: A fragrant double; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Fan the roots and set the crown just at the surface. Fragrant double bubblegum-pink flowers with a green throat bloom in midsummer. Deadhead daily and divide the clump when flowering thins.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for the darkest petals.

directions: Set the crown at grade and mulch to keep roots cool. Deep purple flowers with a yellow-green throat and gold-edged petals bloom in midsummer. Remove spent blooms daily and cut scapes when done.

planting.note: One of the whitest daylilies; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep moist while establishing. Near-white flowers with a green throat open in early to midsummer. Deadhead daily to showcase the pale color and cut scapes after bloom.

planting.note: Grown as much for foliage; morning sun keeps variegation crisp.

directions: Set the crown at grade in soil that stays lightly moist. Foliage is boldly striped green and creamy white, topped by tawny-orange flowers in summer. Remove any all-green shoots that appear to keep the variegation strong.

planting.note: The naturalized ditch daylily; spreads by roots, not seed.

directions: Plant divisions at grade in almost any soil, in sun or light shade. This tough sterile species carries rusty-orange trumpets on tall branched scapes in midsummer and spreads by underground runners. Edge or contain it where you need to limit its vigorous spread.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for bloom the first summer.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it lightly moist at room temperature until germination. Plant out in sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid overwatering. This selection carries larger, deeper lavender-blue flowers than the type; deadhead spent heads to prolong the summer display.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or direct-sow after frost danger passes.

directions: Press the seed onto the surface of a gritty mix and cover only lightly, keeping it just moist until it germinates. Set plants in full sun in poor, well-drained soil where they will tolerate drought once established. The flowers are white with a contrasting deep purple-blue eye; snip stems in the morning for long-lasting cut or dried displays.

planting.note: Begin indoors before the last frost, or division of established clumps in spring.

directions: Surface-sow and lightly cover the seed, then keep it evenly moist until seedlings emerge in two to three weeks. Grow in full sun in lean, sharply drained ground and water sparingly. This white-flowered form produces papery ivory blooms all summer that dry well for everlasting arrangements.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for a compact plant that flowers its first year.

directions: Barely cover the seed and germinate warm, transplanting once seedlings have a few true leaves. This selection is more compact and uniform than the wild species, making it tidy at the front of a sunny, dry border. Expect a steady flush of blue daisy-like blooms from early summer; deadhead to keep them coming.

planting.note: Sow indoors before the last frost for compact plants that bloom the first summer.

directions: Press seed onto the surface, cover lightly, and keep warm and just moist until it sprouts. Plant into full sun in fast-draining, unfertile soil and let it dry between waterings. This dwarf white counterpart offers clean ivory flowers on short, sturdy stems ideal for tight sunny spaces.

planting.note: Start in pots for the rock garden and set out after frost; benefits from a cool spell before sowing.

directions: Sow into a very gritty mix and cover only lightly, keeping cool and just moist until germination. This dwarf Spanish species forms a tight rosette suited to troughs, scree, and rock gardens with sharp drainage. Short-stemmed yellow flowers appear over the low cushion in late spring and summer; avoid winter wet, which is its main enemy.

planting.note: Direct-sow into warm, well-drained soil around the last frost date.

directions: Scatter seed on warm, gritty soil and rake in lightly, thinning seedlings once they establish. Grow this Mediterranean annual in full sun with minimal water, as it thrives in poor dry ground. Small clear-yellow flowers with papery bracts open through summer and self-sow readily in mild sites.
